speaker: interviewer
text: You were talking about Krakow.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Krakow was very exciting, because people came back from Russia, from deep in Siberia-- everywhere. From every parts they were sent during the war. And they came back. They realize the only place is to be in a big city so they will be safe. And Krakow was beautiful. It was the only city that wasn't bombed during the war. It was a beautiful-- it was very historic. The kings of Poland once lived there, and the Wawel, which is the palace, is there. And what's more interesting for the Jewish people, the Remuh Shul is still standing there. The Remuh Shul is 5-- about somewhere like 500 years. It was built in the middle-- like 15 something hundreds. Many hundreds of years it's standing. It's the oldest, most historic shul standing in Krakow. And I was living not far from there, so that I could walk around. And I remember going to the Wawel and-- and experiencing this beautiful city after the war. This was a very great experience, because, you know, this is the most beautiful city in Poland, the most cultured before the war-- one of the most cultured city and the most beautiful. And there were the parks, and I could walk in the parks, and I took pictures I have today, in the winter, in the summer and-- it was exciting. It was an excitement like no other city after the war. And I was glad that I could be in that city, not in my-- scared in my village where I could be killed. You know, I felt safe and beautiful in the most gorgeous-- I realized it was the most-- anybody who was there realized that you are in the most beautiful city. And the one that wasn't bombed, so you could see-- you could feel the-- the-- you know, the ambience that was before the war. In other words, whatever was be-- the way it looked before the war, that's the way it looked. Except that the Jewish people, most of them were gone, except the remnants came back there. So they would go there or to Lodz. Those were the big cities.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did it feel to make friends?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was-- it wasn't so easy because I didn't have the skills right away, how to speak to others. But it came slowly. I-- I didn't speak much, but we all had this, some of us. Some of us had bigger problem because-- I had more because I wasn't hidden. So if you were hidden, you had less problem as far as, you know, getting along with children, how to-- you know-- how to communicate, how to-- they may have had some schooling.

speaker: interviewer
text: I didn't have anything. I was like a little wild animal let out and suddenly made tame. Now how do-- how does a child that didn't have any skills suddenly have to act normal? Can you understand? Besides, I had-- the trauma was in me. It was constantly with me. The trauma of me surviving, losing the family, was terrible. It was constantly with me.

speaker: interviewee
text: But on the surface, I tried to-- suddenly, you know, I was an amazing little girl that I-- I didn't show the-- the abnormality in me. But I tried to act-- you know, in other words, we all tried our best to-- to come slowly back to normal. But it wasn't easy.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did they send you at all to school? Did they start teaching anybody?

speaker: interviewee
text: Later. But first, we start teaching us-- the-- the-- it wasn't time enough. It wasn't long enough. It was only a few months, half a year or so. So there wasn't time enough to-- to establish schools and things. But they tried to teach us, to get us slowly back to what we were, who we were, you understand. And the children, some of them were not reachable because they were with-- non-- every one of these children were either hidden or they were with Polish, you know, with Catholics. So they-- they had to-- they had to take time till they come back, till they realize who you are. So it wasn't-- it wasn't so simple like you think.

speaker: interviewer
text: Anyway, so it's like trying to unite a nation. Like you come to Israel from all parts of the world, and you try to make it into one. And this wasn't easy, because each child had a different background during the war, you understand? You-- you had to-- we had to be molded into slowly to becoming we, you understand? And we were different ages, so it wasn't a simple task to send-- anyway, this happened a little later. So I was in Krakow and I was introduced to songs, Jewish songs, and slowly, slowly, I realized that I am becoming me. OK? And like I said, it was a gorgeous city, and going even to the Remuh, I didn't know, I didn't appreciate the Remuh shul. I didn't understand what the Remuh shul, because I never learned who the Remuh was. It wasn't till years, when I went back to Poland they under-- first I--

speaker: interviewee
text: Even today I meet people, grown people, women that don't know who Remuh is. And I-- I-- now I know. I have his biography. I know all about him. I saw his shul. Now, when I went back the second time, I knew the greatness, what I saw, the Remuh shul. That time I went in, I didn't understand what the-- who the Remuh was. I never was told who the Remuh shul-- who the Remuh was, what a great man he was.

speaker: interviewer
text: So anyway, so now I know. But that time, I walked in the Remuh shul, I didn't know. I remember a girl took me there, and she said, and I didn't understand. She maybe knew more. I didn't. OK? So being living there, finally, one day-- a lot of things happened to me. I went to another home, and I didn't like it. It was too quiet. Coming from Krakow, I didn't like it. It was in-- it was in the-- in [INAUDIBLE]. Not [INAUDIBLE]. It was [? Krynica. ?] It was a very beautiful part of Poland, and-- and this home was supposed to be top notch. I didn't like it, because it was too quiet. Coming from Krakow, I felt like I was suddenly shut in a place. It was too quiet. I needed excitement, because I had so much excitement going, coming, on my own. I couldn't be shut in all of a sudden. It was too quiet. It was like being put in prison. I couldn't suddenly turn off and have no-- and all everything should be so normal that it was too, too, to normal, you understand?

speaker: interviewee
text: And I decided I want to go back to Krakow. And that's where I felt happy. I felt there was everything there. There was the excitement of going to Israel, the excitement. I saw trains. I saw buses. I saw people coming. I saw the culture. It was-- that's what I needed. You know, it was too quiet in the country for me.

speaker: interviewer
text: After being, you know wondering so. It was just too normal, you understand? It was too normal. I wasn't used to being so normal. And so I had to go back to Krakow, and I did. And when I decided I want to do something, I did it. And I went back to Krakow. And I managed to get there.

speaker: interviewee
text: Even though that home was supposed to be the best of all, and yet I didn't like it. I liked the Krakow home, where there was excitement. You walked out, you knew your-- your-- you saw everything. You know, one time I would go into the-- like, give an example, I would go and I saw sliding, the sled. I never was on a sled, so I borrowed the sled, and I-- and I-- and I made a picture of the-- with the sled. And that's what I can show you, how I looked on the sled. Because there was so much going on there that it was unbelievable. And if you went in the park in the summer, you saw a bear standing in the middle of the street. And he would take a picture with the bear. And I never saw a bear, you know, not alive and not a-- not a fake one. And this was going on constantly. You walked and you went [INAUDIBLE], which were the beautiful parks in-- in the center of Krakow. And everything was so exciting. I loved the city of Krakow. But one day came a man. And since I already realized I must listen and I must do what I was told, what was the best-- man came. I-- I don't know if he was a chaplain. He lives in Israel, and I haven't gotten in touch with him. He-- he was doing-- he was working with children who survived and he was bringing them here, bringing them there. He was-- he was involved with Jewish children. And he came and picked me up. He was told-- I was told that he's taking me to-- to [INAUDIBLE], to Zabrze. I didn't even remember for years where this was. It was a little vill-- suburb near Katowice, not far from Katowice, in the suburbs. Zabrze, a small, little, like a sort of village. You know, it looks like a village, but, you know, small houses. And this Zabrze, there was a-- a home for children. It was a very well run-- very, very well run. I have to say that that home was the first time I realized after the war that I belonged there. I felt like I really belonged there, that I-- this is where I belong temporarily. In other words, that I must never run away from there. In other words, that there's nothing better waiting for me. In other words, he told me on the plane-- on the train, he says, you mustn't run away. He already knew my-- what I went through, so he realized I was no ordinary child, that I had went through tremendous things. And he talked to me. And he made me feel so good that I never forgot. He-- he said to me, you must stay, and they're going to treat you wonderful. He got through to me. He got through to me. The way he spoke to me, I realized that this man was special. He was--

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: You know, my friend remembers and I can't remember. I-- sometimes I have-- I wrote it down, but I can't remember right now. But I will-- I know I can reach him, because one time I called him up from Israel, and I reminded him who I was. He didn't remember me. And I couldn't get to him in Israel, where he lives. He's an elderly man now. And he-- I think he had many pictures of many children that he touched their lives. He had a whole album. He was a very special man. I for-- I can't remember right now, but my friend knows the name. And I wrote it down, but I haven't got it in front of me.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you believe him when he told you can't run away and it will be good?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. 100% I believed him. I never did. In other words, when he spoke to me, I realized he reached me. He reached-- he got so close to-- to my inner being that I realized that this man is genuinely interested in me. In other words, that he has my interest.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did it remind you of your grandmother telling you?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, nobody. Not at all, not-- nobody. But he was a unique person. I realize he was dressed in a uniform. He was a person who's living in today after the war, and he had to do his duty. He had a duty to do and he felt he did it well. He did it very well. And he-- he brought me to this home, and I felt like I came temporarily home. Because there-- there was something so unique. The minute I entered the home, I remember I was given a bed, and I felt like I belonged there. And I started to talk. I started to speak to the person next to me. And I told her the whole half a night I was up just telling her. And she was telling me the other half the night. And we didn't go to sleep. Most of the night we didn't sleep because we-- we-- we-- we told-- talked to each other what we went through. She went through very different than I did. And I realized I wasn't the only child that I survived such terrible, went through such-- she went through even more amazing. And I realized that I was-- that I-- whatever she went through is even more amazing. And we talked and talked and talked. In the morning, I realized this is it, that I'm never going to run. And in the morning, I was given soap and a towel-- the first time my own soap and my own towel. Felt good. I can wash. I had to go through the rigors of what everybody-- you know. Washing my face, brushing my teeth, getting my own soap, and going-- living like a normal human being. The first time, like a normal child. And I didn't feel like a child anymore, but I-- I had to start re-- resuming the role of a child, what I was, a 12-year-old. And there were various ages there. And there-- we had-- there was a few rooms, bedrooms, and-- and-- and I remember there was an office and there was the lady one day called me in. And she said, if you want, you can pick up this telephone. And I felt very special, because nobody else was called in this office, all by myself. And she said to me, sit down, please. Very nice, very polite and very-- she knew how to talk to a girl like me. Somehow she made a good impression. And she wasn't like-- sometimes, you know, I met some people, they talked, like, superior. But she was very nice. She didn't talk like a superior. She talked, like, to me.

speaker: interviewer
text: And she said, I want you to know, if you want, you can pick up this telephone and you can call your father. They already knew my history, that I had a father. Because they asked every child, do you have somebody? And I-- I had a father in America. But he could have been on the moon, as far as I-- and I didn't know his exact address. But I knew he was in New York somewhere, and I knew his name. And that was enough. That's all you need. And she said, you can call him if you want to. And I said nothing. I didn't [INAUDIBLE]. I didn't respond. I didn't say nothing. Just sat there without-- without response. That's the way-- I didn't always know how to respond to people, because I had to learn this, you know, how to speak, how to express myself, you understand? I forgot how to express myself to-- to older person. My-- my-- let's say, a person, a young girl who was my age, I could relate to her more, but to her, I did not know how to respond. So I didn't say anything. I just listened, and I just took it in. But I understood what she was saying. But I'd never used a telephone before, and I slowly-- things start to sink that I have a father, that I could eventually get to him. But it was unreachable at that moment, you understand? In other words, I had to go through a lot of things before I realized a lot of-- anyway. So slowly, you know, things got more and more normal every day. One day they said we're gonna go to school. We started to go to school. And, you know, the first time I went to a classroom, and the classroom happened to be half German children, because this was an area where a lot of mixed German and Polish people lived. That was the area, [INAUDIBLE]. There was-- that area, there was one time it was German, one time it was Poland. So there were a lot of half mixed, and they were very antisemitic. I remember that I was sitting in the class and they made me feel like I'm a f-- stranger among them. I do remember that. We had to go to that school. There was no Jewish school. So we-- there were a few Jewish children in each class, and I remember I felt very strange among them. They were blonde-- and not only because they were, but they made us-- I was aware that I was Jewish and they were not. And they made me feel very uncomfortable. OK?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was not long. I wasn't-- and even that thing-- that experience didn't last long, because soon my situation changed. But what happened, I was about to go to school, but it didn't take long and I had to interrupt that part of it. Because then I was told that Rabbi Schonfeld arrived from London, and he was gathering children from all parts of Poland. And-- and we were chosen, six children from the home, that we will go to London to get closer to the West-- out of Poland. That was very important. Because my friend told me that somebody was trying to shoot into the home and even, believe it or not, some were trying to kill-- shoot into the home. In other words, kill a few Jewish children. I don't remember this incident, but my friend said she remembers that. And they had-- it was very scary. In other words, Jewish children suddenly realized that people saw that even Jewish children weren't safe after the war. And it was important to get out, to get the children out that did survive out as soon as possible. So we were chosen priority, six from this home. Why, I don't know exactly. I have a feeling maybe because I had a father and the others didn't have anybody, so they took priorities. They-- these were priority children. And there were various ages, from, I don't know, nine to-- I don't know exact ages. But I was at that time-- it was in '45. No, this was the end of '45, so I must have been like-- 12 and 1/2. 12 and 1/2. I was 12 and 1/2. So there were various ages among the ones, the six-- under 10, 12, 13, 14, you know. Because you can see on the-- if you look at the picture where I am with them, you can see we're each different-- different heights, and you can see that we are different ages. It's very clear to see. And so we six were chosen. There was two sisters, a little boy who's very bright for his age, even smarter than I was. I thought I was smart. He was brighter than me. He was so bright that we-- we thought he was genius. He was a genius. He was only maybe seven or eight at that time, under 10, and he was like a genius. So you talk about smart children surviving, he was even smarter than any of us. And we thought he was brilliant. And he-- we always laughed when he spoke. And so he and a little girl, [? Sasha, ?] she was very blond. And she didn't remember who her parents were because she was given into a Polish family to survive, and her parents didn't survive. And the-- the woman forgot-- the woman that she was by forgot her-- her-- her name she was born with, and till today, she still-- she would like to know, but she doesn't know who her-- her real name was before the war. She was hidden by a non-Jewish.

speaker: interviewer
text: So she was chosen-- a little beautiful, blonde little girl. She was the smallest, I think, one of the smallest from the six. And there was Eva Lieber-- she is today Eva Lieberman, and she was-- she had a little sister. They were both brunettes, dark, and-- and that blonde little [? Sasha. ?] And there was another blonde girl. She was about my age. And she-- we six were chosen.

speaker: interviewee
text: So-- so here comes the adventure, out of Poland. So we were told that we're gonna-- a certain day, we're going to go on a train and we're going to go to the-- to the city of Warsaw, which was the capital once but it was completely demolished, 90%. It was bombed during the war. And when we came, there was nothing but rubble. When we came to that city, I didn't realize it was so completely bombed that we walked on-- on bricks. Demolished, like after the-- a war, you know, if ever saw a-- everything was bombed. It was destroyed by-- both by the bombs and by the uprising. So there was two things that demolished that city. 90% I was heard. 90% was destroyed.

speaker: interviewer
text: So only certain outskirts-- in the outskirts there were maybe some homes left. But the center was-- especially where the-- where the ghetto, where they put the Jewish people during the war, that was the most devastated. You know? And it was in that part that we found-- that they found a house for us. And that's why this was so-- so completely ruined. It was in ruins.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we had to sleep there. But since they realized so many children cannot sleep all in the same-- in this same house, they divided us. They-- some people there was came forward, and they approached Jewish people that survived. This doctor-- we were lucky to go to this Dr. [? Pupka. ?] We set to sleep a few nights in his house. We was-- I don't remember on what we slept, but we slept on the floor. I remember-- how we got so many mattresses, six, I don't know. I don't know. All I remember, we slept on the floor. And we-- we found-- and they made us feel so-- so warm that I haven't met anybody like that. It's such warm, such love they showed to us. And they-- in their minds, they adopted us, because they had no children. So when they came, we-- the few days we spent we felt like we were part of their family. They made us feel so specials. They took many pictures of us. They had a whole album. Many years later when I visited the house, they had a whole album of us children. And I have to tell you, mention something, an incident. When I came to their house, when I finally found them after many years, the husband died already, and just a few months before I came.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when I came to pay-- there was-- people came to pay a shiva call, and there-- somebody asked about me, who was that? They didn't know me. So the-- Mrs. [? Pupka ?] said, this is one of our children. That's how she described about me. They thought of us like their-- one of their children.

speaker: interviewee
text: When they eventually got to Israel, they had one child-- one daughter-- and that's it. So they kept a whole album of pictures. I have only a few, but they had a whole album of us. I just want to mention the people that made an impact on our lives right after the war. These were kind people, wonderful people that-- that you don't see today.

speaker: interviewer
text: People don't make in their lives room for such things. In other words, they don't find room in their house for six children on the floor. This was something I have to mention, how people acted after the war, the compassion they had for others. You understand? Because people could learn today, including me, that what it means to have compassion for somebody less fortunate.

speaker: interviewee
text: They made room on the floor. They had two rooms, but they made room for six children. They realized how-- what we went through, and they didn't want us to sleep in that house, in a cold house. It was unheated. And in this house it was warm, and they took us every day in the parks, and they showed us Warsaw, the way it looked and that.

speaker: interviewer
text: So the neck-- one day we had to leave the house. We were sorry to say goodbye, and we-- we went back to the house where everybody gathered, where everybody from all parts of Poland finally arrived and Rabbi Schonfeld gathered them into one place. From there we slept one night. And in order-- in the morning, we should go by plane to-- to Gdansk. Gdansk was the port. Did Rabbi Schonfeld talk to the children?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. He was a big-- he not only talked, he sang for us. He talked to us. He-- he made himself very visible and-- and every child he knew by name. He was such an unbelievable person, this rabbi. I don't know where he had this-- this drive to do all this, what he did. He gathered money and he came to Poland after the war. I was told he took three transports. But he did this before the war, too. He brought many children to-- before the war from Germany. And-- and some-- I met this week a man who I never spoke to, and he said that he belonged-- he had a school, Rabbi Schonfeld. He had a-- his own school. And-- and this man that I spoke to-- he was from Germany-- and he was sent by his parents to the Rabbi Schonfeld school.

speaker: interviewer
text: So he was-- he was connected. He was-- not only did he bring children, but he-- he was involved with children who came the last transport out of Europe before the war. And after the war he continued the same work. And he was a chief rabbi once in [INAUDIBLE]. Anyway, he was a very big personality, and he touched my life, too. So when he came, he gathered us children. And he couldn't talk to every child, but eventually he did meet all the children on the ship when he had time. Because he was very busy doing the work, what he did in order to take these children out. To get-- you needed permission. You needed a boat. You needed-- it was tremendous amount of work, was involved in this.

speaker: interviewee
text: And he did-- he was super human. That's all I can say. He was super human. He was dressed as a-- like an UNRA. He was dressed in an official uniform, and he would-- made a very imposing figure. When you saw this man, you had respect for him, because he was a good-looking man, tall and very imposing. When he put on the uniform, he looked like a general. And he was so kind to the children. He was so involved. Every child's name meant something to him. He knew every one by heart, years later. He gathered us. We gathered in Gdansk. We stayed in this hotel. In the morning we left for the boat, or ship. I would call it a ship, because-- but it was a small ship. It was big enough to go in the ocean, but it was a small ship. And it was very-- it-- it-- the waves, I was told by my friend-- my friend Eva-- that when we would go on the ship, she was sure we were going to drown. Because the ship was such-- there were such waves, tremendous waves, she thought that we're never going to make that-- that trip. We're not--

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you feel?

speaker: interviewee
text: I didn't-- I was in bed giving up. I couldn't even get off the bed. I was so seasick that I didn't even know what was happening. All I was laying on the bunk. That's all. I didn't know. I-- it was blocked-- I was-- I think that whole journey was blacked out of my-- because I was sick and I was laying on the bunk. I couldn't get off. You know, I was that seasick. And I was laying on the bunk. And I do remember, when we finally could get up on the deck, sometimes, you know, I remember because I got on the deck because I have a picture of it, because I-- they took a picture of me, and I'm on the deck. So you can see that that day I was functioning. But part of that journey, I was out. Because the waves, she told me, was so big. And we-- by the way, we left Friday afternoon. It was erev Shabbos. She says, we were Shabbos on the boat-- on the ship. And she thought-- I didn't remember any of that. She remembered, my friend. She was [INAUDIBLE]. And she-- and she said she never thought we're going to-- we're going to survive this trip. But it did. It made it. And we arrived in London, you know, erev Pesach. We arrived erev Pesach. By the way, I'm going back a little bit. When I left Zabrze, that home, that was the first time that I celebrated Purim. I forgot to mention. It was the first time that I celebrated Purim in a long, many years. And I have a picture of it sitting at the table, the picture on Purim. And there were fruits on the table, and fruits wasn't so easily available in Poland. But there were oranges, I think, and you can see on the picture, and there were fruits. And-- and we were celebrating in the very best way, I think, the-- on the best that we could under those circumstances. And we were like a happy little family, because all the children, we had one thing in common. We all survived, and some of us less the parents. And it was a very happy Purim because-- and that was the Purim-- the first Purim that I-- that I remember since I was a child. I don't even remember before, because Purim wasn't that big. But that was a Purim I remember I celebrated. And I remember it vividly. And Pesach, from-- we were in London. And it was the first time I remember celebrating Pesach since I left my mother, since I celebrated Pesach with my mother. It was the first Pesach that I remember eating matzos and celebrating in normal-- under normal conditions, as-- as a normal child. The first normal, in other words, feeling like the closest to a normal child. And I remember they made us feel so good when we came to London. The people were so kind. They were so compassionate. More compassion than in America, and I saw the difference. And I hated-- I wanted to go back to Europe when I saw-- when I met the people in America, I didn't like them. Because I realized that the people in London were much more compassionate. They showed us understanding. They made us feel good, as good as you could make any child feel. We were invited for Shabbos in London. Many a Shabbos I would go to this family, Levy. They had this-- he was a dentist-- a doctor. He was a dentist. And he-- there was such a normal-- there was such normal life in there that I never seen this in all the years since the war. There were flowers on the-- I never-- I hadn't seen this since the war. I mean, which-- the man, the husband brought flowers every-- and Shabbos was such a beautiful thing in that home. Everyone was so normal.

speaker: interviewer
text: We're going to change the tape now.
